text: A clip is placed on the short gastric vessels near the spleen. The surgeon uses blunt dissection to carefully separate tissues in preparation for using an energy device to detach the attachments.

The surgeon identifies dense adhesions between the fundus of the stomach and the spleen, as well as between the spleen and the diaphragm. Large short gastric vessels are clipped to prevent post-operative bleeding, followed by cutting them with an energy device.

The surgeon uses a right-angle instrument to carefully dissect tissues near the fundus of the stomach, ensuring no thermal injury occurs during the process. The viability of the stomach fundus is checked multiple times to prevent any accidental burns.

The surgeon has mobilized the stomach and fundus, and after taking down the short gastric vessels, enters the lesser sac. The pancreas is visible with the pulsating splenic artery above it. The splenic artery is dissected and clipped to prevent bleeding during hilum dissection, and a locking clip is applied. The artery is then divided using a stapling device with a one millimeter closed staple height.

The surgeon exposes and dissects the splenocolic ligament using an energy device, carefully avoiding the lower pole vessels. The spleen's segmental blood supply is visible, with distinct vascular structures at the lower pole, upper pole, and hilum.

The surgeon uses an energy device to completely surround and cauterize vessels near the spleen, maintaining a distance of one to two centimeters from the spleen to prevent major bleeding. Care is taken to avoid injury to the tail of the pancreas, which may be located within the hilum of the spleen.

The surgeon carefully dissects the splenorhinal ligament while lifting the spleen towards the abdominal wall. The splenic artery, splenic vein, and splenic hilum are visible, with no apparent pancreatic tail in the region. The surgeon considers two methods for addressing the splenic hilum: taking it en bloc with both the splenic artery and vein simultaneously.

The surgeon attempts to dissect across the hilum of the spleen, encountering difficulty in accessing the area behind the hilum. A Raytech sponge is placed nearby as a precaution for potential bleeding. The surgeon uses a large right-angle instrument to try to encompass all the vessels, but struggles to safely reach across them.

The surgeon abandons the previous approach and begins dissecting the splenic artery and veins separately within the splenic hilum. A branch of the splenic artery is isolated, clipped, and divided using a ligature device, with no proximal clipping due to the absence of pulsations. The ligature device seals the non-pulsating vessel, and another arterial branch to the spleen is visible during the ongoing dissection.

The surgeon identifies a pulsating vessel, clips it twice on the proximal side and twice on the splenic side, then divides it using an energy device. The splenic vein is then stapled and transected using a stapler with a one millimeter closed staple height. The area is inspected for hemostasis after the procedure.

The surgeon uses an energy device to dissect the front splenic ligament, carefully manipulating the large spleen adhered to the diaphragm. A centimeter ridge of tissue is left on the spleen to aid in grasping without tearing the capsule. The remaining portion of the splenorhinal ligament is visualized and dissected with the energy device, ensuring no injury to underlying structures. After mobilizing the spleen and removing attachments and blood supply, the spleen is moved aside, and the surgical field is irrigated to inspect for hemostasis.
